coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of fluorescent poly(α-cyclodextrin)/carbon quantum dots composite for efficient removal and detection of toluene and xylene from aqueous media

Pina, João
Pereira, Rui F.P.
Murtinho, Dina
Valente, Artur J.M.
Fajardo, André R.
coredata.dc:description:
This study reports the synthesis and characterization of a supramolecular composite comprised of carbon dots (CDots) embedded within net-poly[(α-cyclodextrin)-ν-(citric acid)] (α-CD/CA/CDots) for the removal and detection of toluene and xylene from aqueous media. The remarkable stability of CDots within the composite enables the preservation of photoluminescence properties for prolonged storage and extended UV-light irradiation. As demonstrated, following the adsorption of both organic compounds, the composite detected them in the aqueous medium due to a fluorescence quenching mechanism. Spectroscopic analyses reveal that the accessible Stern–Volmer quenching constants for toluene and xylene are KSVa = 15.4 M−1 and KSVa = 10.3 M−1, respectively. As a result, the α-CD/CA/CDots composite were sensitive to the tested volatile organic compounds (LODtoluene = 3.7 mg/L and LODxylene = 4.9 mg/L). Optimal conditions for toluene and xylene adsorption were found, allowing to achieve noticeable adsorption capabilities (q
e(toluene) = 68.9 and q
e(xylene) = 48.2 mg/g) and removal efficiencies exceeding 70%. Different characterization techniques confirmed the successful synthesis of the composite and elucidated the interaction mechanisms between the adsorbent and the tested compounds. In summary, the multifunctionality demonstrated by the α-CD/CA/CDots composite ranks it as an efficient and promising adsorbent and detection probe for this class of water contaminants.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
This study reports the synthesis and characterization of a supramolecular composite comprised of carbon dots (CDots) embedded within net-poly[(α-cyclodextrin)-ν-(citric acid)] (α-CD/CA/CDots) for the removal and detection of toluene and xylene from aqueous media. The remarkable stability of CDots within the composite enables the preservation of photoluminescence properties for prolonged storage and extended UV-light irradiation. As demonstrated, following the adsorption of both organic compounds, the composite detected them in the aqueous medium due to a fluorescence quenching mechanism. Spectroscopic analyses reveal that the accessible Stern–Volmer quenching constants for toluene and xylene are KSVa = 15.4 M−1 and KSVa = 10.3 M−1, respectively. As a result, the α-CD/CA/CDots composite were sensitive to the tested volatile organic compounds (LODtoluene = 3.7 mg/L and LODxylene = 4.9 mg/L). Optimal conditions for toluene and xylene adsorption were found, allowing to achieve noticeable adsorption capabilities (q
e(toluene) = 68.9 and q
e(xylene) = 48.2 mg/g) and removal efficiencies exceeding 70%. Different characterization techniques confirmed the successful synthesis of the composite and elucidated the interaction mechanisms between the adsorbent and the tested compounds. In summary, the multifunctionality demonstrated by the α-CD/CA/CDots composite ranks it as an efficient and promising adsorbent and detection probe for this class of water contaminants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are air pollutants that have become commonplace and pose a substantial risk to humans and the environment. Due to this impact, the development of materials for the removal and detection of VOCs becomes of paramount importance(Isinkaralar and Meruyert, 2023; Zhang et al., 2022). This justifies the number of papers, published in recent years, describing new highly adsorptive detectors (Azhagapillai et al., 2020; Siu et al., 2023). The development of methodologies for the adsorption of VOCs is also suitable for various industrial sectors so that they can regulate themselves in terms of air and water quality control. Concerning organic pollutants, recovery techniques are considered more advantageous and energetically more efficient when compared to techniques that promote the degradation of the chemical structure and the putative production of by-products (potentially toxic). Among adsorbents, the use of biomass as an adsorbent appears to be an effective way of removing and controlling VOCs pollution (Huang et al., 2023; Wei et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2023).

Environmental remediation can also be seen as prevention. The detection of VOCs can be the key to pre-testing industrial waste before it reaches the environment. In general, an adsorbent material with detection capability can be useful in several ways. A wide range of materials are using fluorescent probes to detect contaminants. Cao et al. (2020) report a formaldehyde and acetaldehyde detection system using 2,5-dihydroxy-p-benzenedicarbonamide as a fluorescent probe. Ennis et al. (2023) have recently prepared quantum dots-based probes for the detection of homemade explosives. Although the applied analytical techniques have a high sensitivity, they have a high operating cost due to reagents and the need for various operating steps, without considering the shortcomings in the aspect of immediate visual detection. Therefore, to develop materials possessing dual function, easy applicability, and immediate detection is demanding. In the context of low cost and high efficiency, polymeric materials are those that best meet those properties in terms of versatility and stability. Polymeric matrices are ideal structures for anchoring fluorescent particles such as carbon quantum dots. Studies have shown that in addition to the ability of quantum dots to adhere to the crosslinked structure of polymers, polymers can improve the fluorescence capabilities of quantum dots through the process of surface passivation (Goyal et al., 2023; Koken and Bulbul Sonmez, 2023; Utzeri et al., 2022). The functionalization of poly(oligosaccharides), either as gels or nanosponges, is a current trend in the development of detection systems, taking advantage of the unique properties of these oligosaccharides, including their availability to form host-guest supramolecular interactions with organic molecules or to coordinate with metal ions (Belenguer-Sapiña et al., 2021).

Among oligosaccharides, cyclodextrins are amphiphilic macromolecules due to its hydrophobic cavity and hydrophilic outer surface. The hydrophobic cavity enables the thread of a broad range of hydrophobic guests such as surfactants (Fang and Bhandari, 2010) or aromatic compounds (Gim et al., 2017), forming highly stable host-guest complexes, and contributing for the improvement of guest properties, including solubility enhancement and volatility and antioxidant control (Aytac et al., 2018; Wadhwa et al., 2017).

Cyclodextrin-based polymers emerged with the need to develop materials that combine the advantages of cyclodextrins, namely the possibility of establishing inclusion complexes with numerous molecules, with the facility of removal and recycling after use, since the inclusion process can be reversible. The obtainment of crosslinked and water-insoluble materials, in addition to the possibility to introduce new functionalities in CDs, specifically through the use of different crosslinkers, has allowed the development of a smorgasbord of new materials with applications ranging from pharmaceuticals to environmental remediation (Cova et al., 2021, 2018).

In this contribution, we present the development of a material comprising an α-cyclodextrin-based polymer, prepared using citric acid (CA) as a crosslinker and doped with carbon quantum dots (α-CD/CA/CDots). This material serves for quality control and the adsorption of volatile organic compounds in scenarios requiring their determination. The detection platform consists of a fluorescent polymer with an optically active surface designed to adsorb and, through this interaction, detect xylene and toluene species in aqueous solutions. The material design was selected based on extensive synthesis and morphological, physicochemical, and analytical studies. This assembly offers sensitivity, high reusability, and selectivity in the process of adsorption and detection of harmful pollutants such as xylene and toluene. The presence of these organic compounds in water poses risks such as hydrocarbon pneumonitis. Chronic overexposure can lead to degenerative diseases of the central nervous system (CNS) and other target organ effects. The volatile nature of these compounds makes their removal and, more importantly, quantification in the environment a significant challenge, especially near industrial activities where their presence in water is notable. In this context, the development of technologies capable of both removing and detecting them is urgently needed.

α-Cyclodextrin (α-CD, >98%) from Acros Organics (USA), citric acid (CA) from Labchem Inc. (USA); poly(ethylene glycol) (M w 800 g mol−1, PEG-800) from Êxodo Científica LTDA (Brazil) and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) from Riedel-de Haën (Germany). For the adsorption/detection experiments, o-xylene (95%) and toluene (96%) from Sigma-Aldrich (USA) were used. All solutions were prepared by using Milli-Q water and methanol (95%, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) in a 3:1 ratio (v/v).

The PEG-800 (3 mL) was dissolved in deionized water (40 mL) in a glass beaker, similar to the methodology described elsewhere (Silva et al., 2024). The solution was transferred into a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated at 140 °C for 24 h, and then cooled to room temperature (Silva et al., 2024). The obtained solution was further subjected to dialysis (24 h, changing the deionized Milli-Q water every 6 h). Finally, a light-yellow aqueous solution containing quantum carbon dots was obtained and labeled as CDots. The entire procedure was carried out in an inert N2 atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of α-CD/CA and α-CD/CA/CDots composites
ce:section.ce:para: Crosslinked α-CD was synthesized by polycondensation of citric acid (CA) with α-CD. (Liu et al., 2019; Silva et al., 2024). In short, α-CD (1.5 g), CA (0.75 g), KH2PO4 (0.37 g), and 30 mL of deionized water were mixed in a round bottom flask and stirred at 30 °C for 1 h. The mixture was transferred to a Petri dish and heated in a drying oven (120 °C) for 3 h. After cooling, the formed material was washed with deionized water several times for removal any reagent in excess, filtered, and vacuum-dried at 60 °C overnight. The obtained material is labeled as α-CD/CA. For the preparation of CDots containing α-cyclodextrin (α-CD/CA/CDots), a similar procedure was carried out by replacing the deionized water with the aqueous quantum dots dispersion, described in the previous section (2.2).

The chemical nature of the CDots solution and the prepared materials was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) coupled to an ATR module in a Shimadzu IR-Affinity-1 spectrometer (Japan) in a wavelength range of 4000–400 cm−1. Thermal degradation studies were performed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Netzsch Instruments, model TG209–F3 Tarsus, USA), using 3 mg of each sample at 20 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min in an inert atmosphere, and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) with power compensation (PerkinElmer, model DSC 7, USA).

N2 sorption and desorption analyses were performed a Micrometrics ASAP 2000 (USA) equipment to evaluate the specific surface area of the adsorbents. ζ potential measurements were carried out using 1 mL of α-CD/CA and a dispersion of α-CD/CA/CDots in 0.01 mmol/L KCl aqueous solution at 25 °C (Malvern Zetasizer NanoZS, USA).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Gemini 2-Zeiss, Merlin-Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) micrographs were taken at 10.0 kV and 100 or 10000 × magnification to analyze the morphology of α-CD/CA and α-CD/CA/CDots before and after contact with aqueous solutions of toluene and xylene at 100 mg/L. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on a JEOL JEM 2100 microscope (Japan) at 200 kV. The samples were previously dispersed in water and then placed on analysis grids (UC-A in Holy 400 mesh Cu grids, Ted Pella ref. 01824) by drop-casting, followed by drying at room temperature.

Swelling experiments were performed to evaluate the maximum liquid uptake ability of the prepared composites. For this, the previously weighed materials (md, ca. 20 mg) were placed in an Erlenmeyer and filled with 40 mL of distilled water at 25 °C. After 30 min (equilibrium time), the swollen samples were removed from the Erlenmeyer, excess surface water was removed, and the adsorbent weight (ms) was measured. The maximum degree of swelling, S(%), was calculated using Eq. (1), respectively.

Batch experiments were carried out to evaluate the ability of α-CD/CA and α-CD/CA/CDots to adsorb toluene and xylene from aqueous solutions. To optimize the results, some parameters were preliminarily investigated: adsorbent dosage (solid-liquid ratio, Rs/l, between 1 and 6) and solution pH (4–8). These experiments were done at room temperature (approximately 25 °C) and the following protocol was used: Specific amounts of the dried materials were added to vials filled with aqueous solutions of toluene or xylene (10 mL) at 100 mg/L of initial concentration. Fresh stock solutions of toluene or xylene were prepared using volumetric flasks (250 mL) sealed with rubber septa and filled with distilled water. The pH of each solution was adjusted by using HNO3 (0.1 mol/L) or NaOH (0.1 mol/L) solutions. It is worth noting that after adding the adsorbents, the vials used in the adsorption experiments were hermetically covered with Teflon® film (to avoid volatilization of aromatic compounds). After 30 min, the solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm Nylon filter (LabFil) and aliquots were removed from the vials and at once analyzed by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The adsorption performance of the materials about toluene and xylene was evaluated by calculating the removal efficiency, Q, and the adsorption capacity, q
t (mg/g), using Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively:

e . The quantification of analytes was performed by UV–visible absorption spectrometry, using a Shimadzu UV-2600i (Japan) spectrometer in the wavelength range of 200–350 nm. All experiments reported here were performed in triplicate.

To examine the selective adsorption capacity of the α-CD/CA/CDots composite towards the target VOCs, batch experiments were conducted using binary solutions of toluene and xylene. Briefly, a binary solution comprising 50 mL of distilled water containing 25 mg/L of each compound was prepared and introduced into a 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask. Next, 20 mg of α-CD/CA/CDots was introduced into the Erlenmeyer flask, which was then sealed hermetically with Teflon® film and subjected to orbital agitation at 180 rpm at room temperature for ca. 25 min. After that, the toluene and xylene quantification were performed as previously described. The selectivity parameter (S) was calculated by Eq. (4):

where m a and m b refer to the mass of each adsorbed compound; this procedure was performed in triplicate.

Following the initial use, the reusability of the synthesized composites was assessed by quantifying the desorption of toluene and xylene. After the adsorption experiments, the composite was recovered and subjected to heating in an oven for 1 h to evaporate the contaminants. The quantification of toluene and xylene followed the previously described procedure. The desorption rate (D) was calculated using Eq. (5):
(5)

where q
d is the amount of each VOC desorbed, and q
a denotes the amount adsorbed by the composite. A total of ten consecutive cycles were conducted under the previously mentioned conditions.

Absorbance spectra were acquired using a MacyS1100 (Japan) spectrophotometer with a tungsten lamp source and a silicon photodiode detector. Fluorescence measurements were conducted using a PerkinElmer LS 45 (USA) fluorescence spectrophotometer, equipped with a plotter unit and a quartz cell with a 1 cm optical path.

Batch experiments involving the α-CD/CA/CDots composite in contact with varying concentrations of toluene and xylene solutions were performed to evaluate its capability for its detection. Furthermore, fluorescence stability assays were conducted to evaluate the long-term fluorescence sensor performance of α-CD/CA/CDots when exposed to toluene and xylene solutions over a 20-day period.

Fig. 1 (A) shows the FTIR spectra of the synthesized CDots and composites. Regarding the CDots spectrum, distinct bands at 3420, 2820, and 1096 cm−1 are observable, corresponding to the O–H, C–H, and C–O stretching, respectively. These features closely resemble the peaks seen in the carbon precursors of raw PEG-800 (Jiang et al., 2022). The bands at 1626 and 1721 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching of the CC bonds in CDots structure and the bending of the O–H bonds of water molecules, respectively (Peng et al., 2020).

The FTIR spectrum of α-CD/CA shows noticeable bands at 3390 cm−1, attributed to the O–H stretching of hydroxyl bonds, at 2942 cm−1 due to the asymmetric stretching of C–H bonds, and at 1155 cm−1 referring to glycosidic bonds of α-CD (Rusa and Tonelli, 2000). Additionally, bands at 1734 cm−1 are seen, corresponding to the stretching of CO bonds of ester groups, while the bands at 1078 and 1024 cm−1, attributable to the C–O–C bonds, confirm the crosslinking of α-CD with CA (Junthip, 2019). Our group has previously reported similar findings about the crosslinking of β-CD with CA. (Silva et al., 2024).

In the α-CD/CA/CDots spectrum, the band corresponding to O–H stretching of hydroxyl groups undergoes a shift to a lower wavenumber region (3400 cm−1), suggesting potential interaction between the hydroxyl groups of the CDots and the α-CD/CA matrix. Furthermore, the α-CD/CA/CDots spectrum reveals bands at 2879 cm−1, originating from the C–H stretching of CHx groups associated with CDots. Moreover, a discernible shift to higher wavenumbers is evident in bands originating from the α-CD/CA matrix, specifically at 1630 and 1729 cm−1, attributable to the presence of CDots. The hydroxyl groups in α-CD contribute to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds, fostering interactions with quantum dots. Consequently, the electronegative groups in α-CD/CA demonstrate a preference for interacting with CDots via hydrogen bonding, which explains the shifts observed in the α-CD/CA/CDots spectrum. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that the intensity of the band attributed to the CO stretching of the ester group decreased in comparison to other bands in the same spectrum, which remained unaltered in the α-CD/CA/CDots composite as compared to the α-CD/CA spectrum. This observation suggests that the crosslinking of α-CD with CA decreased in the α-CD/CA/CDots composite due to the presence of CDots.

TEM microscopy images serve as a valuable tool for discerning the presence of CDots in the α-CD/CA matrix. As depicted in Fig. 1(B), the images unveil the abundant existence of spherical nanoparticles, both coalesced and individual, exhibiting a diameter ranging from 3 to 5 nm. Comparable results about the diameter of these nanoparticles synthesized using PEG as a carbon source have been reported by other authors (Silva et al., 2024). High-resolution measurements further discern that these spherical structures have a well-defined crystalline order. This unequivocally verifies the formation of the α-CD/CA/CDots composite, characterized by active sites conducive to adsorption applications and favorable optical properties conducive to fluorescent responses, making it suitable for use as a probe.

To gain insights into the thermal stability of the synthesized composites, thermogravimetric analyses were conducted, and the results are depicted in Fig. 2 . The TGA curve for α-CD exhibited two distinct weight loss stages. The initial stage in the temperature range of 54–80 °C corresponds to the evaporation of adsorbed water. The later stage, which is more pronounced, exhibits its peak degradation temperature at 327 °C, accounting for approximately 81% weight loss attributed to the decomposition of α-CD (Venkatesh et al., 2014). The thermogram for α-CD/CA closely resembles that recorded for α-CD, with two notable distinctions. Firstly, the water loss is scarcely discernible owing to the increased hydrophobic character of the crosslinked material. Secondly, the degradation of its main structure constitutes approximately 46% of the total mass, occurring at a maximum temperature of 323 °C. This suggests a higher level of structural organization and thermal stability as a consequence of the crosslinking process.

In contrast, the TGA curve of α-CD/CA/CDots exhibited evident alterations compared to the composite without CDots. The weight loss stage attributed to water evaporation occurred in a wide temperature range and reached a maximum at 106 °C, causing a weight loss of approximately 20%. This trend indicates that the presence of CDots increases the hydrophilicity of the composite. Additionally, the weight loss stage associated with the decomposition of α-CD/CA shifted to a lower temperature range, with a peak temperature registered at 260 °C. The weight loss computed for this stage was around 46%. This change in the thermal behavior of the α-CD/CA matrix can be attributed to the influence of CDots, which affects the crosslinking process as demonstrated by the FTIR data. Hence, the thermal stability of the α-CD/CA/CDots is impaired (Trotta et al., 2000). Another evidence of the presence of CDots in the composite matrix is the increase in the residual mass at 400 °C compared to the α-CD/CA.

SEM microscopy images were employed to observe changes in the surface morphology of the synthesized composites. Fig. 3 (A) and (B) depict the surface morphology of the α-CD/CA material, featuring well-distributed amorphous granules of varying sizes and a surface containing fissures. It is noteworthy that measurements of DLS revealed an average size of (5.5 ± 1.6) nm for the particles of this material, with a surface area of 1.80 m2/g obtained through N2 adsorption and desorption measurements. In contrast, α-CD/CA/CDots, while also exhibiting an amorphous and irregular morphology (Fig. 3(C) and (D)), present a significantly more homogeneous size distribution (4.1 ± 0.3 nm) according to DLS data. The particles are more dispersed, and the material boasts a higher surface area of 2.85 m2/g. The organization of these materials can be influenced by the dispersion of CDots within the α-CD/CA structure. Considering this information, it is conceivable that the smaller size, larger surface area, and more amorphous morphology with cavities in the α-CD/CA/CDots composite confer advantageous properties for applications as both an adsorbent and a detection probe.

In addition to the physicochemical, morphological, and textural characteristics, another crucial property of an adsorbent intended to remove pollutants from water is its swelling performance. The swelling performance of an adsorbent is crucial because it influences surface area, pore structure, mass transfer, selectivity, regeneration potential, and application flexibility (Pereira et al., 2021; Seida and Tokuyama, 2022). All these factors collectively determine the effectiveness of the adsorbent in removing pollutants from water and contribute to its overall performance in water treatment applications. Considering the composites synthesized in this study, α-CD/CA/CDots demonstrated the highest swelling performance, (179 ± 3)% compared to material α-CD/CA which obtained (124 ± 3)%. This percentage of swelling is related to the amount of liquid that the material can absorb. Higher percentage values allow more pollutants to encounter the active sites of the adsorbent material. Therefore, it is possible to assume that the adsorption rate of the material with a higher swelling rate will be higher. Comparatively, this data demonstrates that the presence of CDots enabled this composite to swell 1.46 times more than α-CD/CA. This disparity between the two composites can be ascribed to the lower degree of crosslinking in the composite, as CDots are known to interfere with the crosslinking process conducted by citric acid(Zhao et al., 2015). FTIR and TGA/DTG data support this inference. Furthermore, this enhanced swelling capability can be attributed to several factors, including the unique physicochemical properties of the composite, increased surface area (as confirmed by textural analysis), and the higher concentration of hydrophilic groups resulting from the presence of CDots. These factors collectively facilitate the entry of water molecules into the composite's pores. The demonstrated enhanced swelling capability of α-CD/CA/CDots holds potential implications for its application in water treatment, as an adsorbent, or in other fields where swelling behavior is a critical parameter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and luminescent properties of α-CD/CA/CDots composite
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we will assess the bifunctionality of α-CD/CA/CDots, serving as both an adsorbent and a probe. Initially, we will elucidate the interaction between toluene and xylene. Subsequently, based on the optimal interaction conditions between the composite and the analyte, we will evaluate its performance as a fluorescent probe.

Preliminary adsorption experiments were conducted to investigate the optimal conditions concerning adsorbent dosage (solid/liquid ratio – Rs/l) and pH values. For both analytes, the q
e values continuously decrease as the dosage of α-CD/CA and α-CD/CA/CDots increases from 10 to 60 mg (Fig. 4 (A) and 4(B)). This trend can be easily attributed to the saturation of the adsorption sites on the materials. Additionally, both materials exhibit superior adsorption capacity for toluene, with α-CD/CA/CDots demonstrating greater adsorption efficiency than the α-CD/CA composite for both adsorbates. Moreover, the highest Q values were 57% for toluene and 45% for xylene in the case of α-CD/CA. In contrast, for α-CD/CA/CDots, the Q values were notably higher, reaching 98% for toluene and 91% for xylene at the highest adsorbent dosage (60 mg).

Adsorption experiments conducted under different pH conditions revealed that this variable has negligible effects on the adsorption of toluene and xylene on both composites (Fig. 4(C) and (D)). Moreover, alterations in the pH caused minimal variations in the adsorption capacities of the α-CD/CA and α-CD/CA/CDots materials. Generally, pH changes can influence the surface charge of the adsorbent through the protonation/deprotonation of functional groups, affecting adsorption positively or negatively (Hosseini et al., 2020). However, the chemical nature of VOCs is minimally affected by changes in the surface charge of the adsorbents. Based on these results, sequential experiments were carried out at pH 6 using R s/l equal to 1 mg/mL, respectively.

The adsorption kinetics were investigated to obtain insights into the adsorption mechanism of toluene and xylene onto α-CD/CA and α-CD/CA/CDots composites. The collected data are depicted in Fig. 5 . From a kinetic point of view, the adsorption of these VOCs on α-CD/CA occurs in a single step, with the q
t values increasing up to the 60 s range for both contaminants, then slowing down before reaching equilibrium. At this condition, the α-CD/CA composite was able to adsorb 35 mg/g of toluene and 21 mg/g of xylene, corresponding to Q values of 30% and 25%, respectively. For α-CD/CA/CDots, the kinetic profile seems to be different, suggesting the occurrence of two adsorption stages. The first one occurs up to approximately 50 s, resulting in adsorption capacities of (69 ± 2) mg/g (Q = 50%) for toluene and (48.2 ± 0.3) mg/g (Q = 45%) for xylene. The second stage takes place subsequently.

To gain a deeper understanding of the interaction mechanism inherent in kinetic profiles, pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order kinetic models (Eqs. (6) and (7), respectively) were employed to fit the experimental kinetic data (Figs. S1 and S2).

In the above equations, k
1 (min−1) and k
2 (g/mg min) are kinetic constants. The fitting adequacy was evaluated by using the Akaike information criterion (AIC) (Matias et al., 2023), computed through the following equation:
(8)

where n is the number of experimental points, s 2 is the residual sum of squares and K is the number of independent parameters of the model. From the analysis of AIC (and determination coefficient, R2, values, as well) reported in Table 1 , the adsorption kinetics of both contaminants on the composites followed the pseudo-second order kinetics, indicating that an interaction between the analytes and the polymeric matrix is occurring. Typically, pseudo-second order kinetics indicate that the adsorption behavior involved chemical adsorption as well as physical adsorption, adsorption data for other organic contaminants agree with other works in the literature (Wang et al., 2014). It is also worth noticing that k
2 is lower for toluene than for xylene, independently of the adsorbent, suggesting that the matrix demonstrates a preference for toluene during the adsorption process from a kinetic point of view.

However, a lingering question remains: what is the role of CDots in the adsorption process of toluene and xylene? To help address this question, the adsorption kinetics have been evaluated using Eq. (9), which considers the simultaneous biphasic adsorption:
(9)

where t 1 and t 2 correspond to the maximum of the first derivative, for each adsorption process of kinetic data and p is the contribution of each adsorbate population. From the best fitting parameters to the data presented in Fig. 5 and Tables 1 and it can be concluded that: (i) the maximum adsorption capacity for the first adsorption stage is higher than for the α-CD/CA; (ii) the second adsorption stage, for both analytes, show a rate constant one order of magnitude lower than those observed for the first stage; and (iii) the second adsorption stage involves 16% and 29% of the maximum adsorbed xylene and toluene, respectively. It seems reasonable to explain some of these results by the modification of the polymeric structure induced by the presence of CDots. For example, in the presence of CDots the polymer volume fraction of the polymeric matrix decreases, due to the increased degree of swelling, increasing the availability of the cyclodextrins to interact with xylene and toluene, thus facilitating the formation of host-guest complexes (Kfoury et al., 2018; Sanemasa et al., 1988). On the other hand, the point (i) can also be explained by the contribution of the CDots to the interaction with the volatile compounds. In fact, dots can interact with aromatic compounds due to the H-bonds formed on their surface and the π electrons present in the core of the CDots (Jain et al., 2009; Shabbir et al., 2023). Therefore, the α-CD/CA/CDots composite offers additional binding sites compared to α-CD/CA. Furthermore, the results can be attributed to the presence of carboxylic groups that interact with VOCs molecules through a π-π electron donor-acceptor mechanism, allowing the occurrence of hydrophobic interactions between them (Shabbir et al., 2023). The lower hydrophobicity of toluene (log K
ow ≈ 2.69) compared to xylene (log K
ow ≈ 2.77), as well as its lower molecular weight can explain the differences in adsorption kinetics, considering that the mobility of toluene is favored. Finally, the second adsorption stage can be justified by the presence of hindered cyclodextrins and/or CDots as elsewhere reported (Silva et al., 2024).

For comparison, Table S1 summarizes the performance of varied materials tested for the adsorption of toluene and xylene from aqueous medium. Our composite material demonstrates a superior adsorption capacity (70 mg/g and 50 mg/g for toluene and xylene, respectively) at neutral pH. Additionally, it is noteworthy that with our adsorbent, the equilibrium condition is achieved after approximately 24 min (see discussion below).

To understand the adsorption process of toluene and xylene on α-CD/CA and α-CD/Cdots, the experimental data were fitted using different isotherm models. Namely, the Langmuir (Eq. (10)), and Langmuir-Freundlich (Eq. (11)) isotherm models were used (Abin-Bazaine et al., 2022; Kyzas et al., 2009).

The parameters q e refer to the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of each adsorbent according to the Langmuir and Langmuir-Freundlich isotherm models. K
L (L/mg) and K
L-F (L/mg) are the constants for Langmuir and Langmuir-Freundlich, respectively, and b is the Langmuir-Freundlich mixing constant. The fitting parameters obtained for both isotherm models are summarized in Table 2 and the plots are presented in Fig. S2. The data obtained and presented here follow the Langmuir–Freundlich isothermal model. This isotherm represents a high equilibrium condition for the complexation and/or adsorption processes of compounds governed by electrostatic attraction.

Among the numerous characteristics necessary for the application of bifunctional materials, the possibility of reuse is the most important in financial and environmental terms(Abdel-Aziz et al., 2019). Reuse cycles can translate into reduced operating costs and minimized pollution generation. To evaluate the regeneration/reuse capacity of the prepared composite (α-CD/CA/CDots), we estimated and compared its adsorption capacity after ten consecutive adsorption/desorption cycles. Moreover, spectroscopy analyses were utilized to shed light on the interactions between the α-CD/CA/CDots composite and the tested VOCs. Fig. 6 (A) to 6(C) display the FTIR spectra of the α-CD/CA/CDots material after the adsorption of toluene and xylene (individually or in a binary system), while Fig. 6(D) and (E) illustrate the q
e and D values computed for toluene and xylene after consecutive reuse cycles.

The FTIR spectra, in Figs. S3(A) and S3(B), for both contaminants show changes in the band at 3426 cm−1 the shift of this band to higher wavelengths indicates the result of hydrophobic interactions between the oxygenated functional groups of the matrix and the contaminants. Furthermore, the increase in the intensity of the bands at 2910 cm−1 is due to the contribution of C–H bonds from both toluene and xylene. The sharp increase in the band at 1617 cm−1 is highlighted, showing the contribution of double bonds between carbons arising from the aromatic ring of the contaminants. Finally, the displacement of the bands at 1117 cm−1 indicates the occupation of the active sites of the adsorbent by contaminants. The same changes were observed in the spectrum recorded from the composited utilized in the adsorption process performed in the binary system (Fig. S3(C)).

Based on the previous discussion and these spectroscopic data, it is believed that over the adsorption time, non-adsorbed molecules can readily interact with the adsorbate monolayer, triggering an additional interaction process that does not depend on the interaction between contaminant sites. Also, π-π interactions take place between aromatic rings of toluene and xylene that function as electron acceptors and functional groups containing oxygen atoms (e.g., hydroxyl and carboxylate present in the composites) on the surface of materials that act as electron donors (Lian et al., 2019). Specifically, for xylene, the methyl groups can also function as an electron donor in the system, making the π-π interaction stronger. In addition to π-π interactions, the tested contaminants can interact with the composites through hydrophobic forces, as reported in other studies (Wang et al., 2018), where the α-CD/CA matrix bonds can directly interact with the contaminants.

The physicochemical features of these two VOCs play a significant role in influencing the mechanism of adsorption between the adsorbent and adsorbate, thereby impacting the overall adsorption performance. This concern became evident upon analysing the results of the adsorption experiment conducted with the binary mixture of toluene and xylene. The computed Q values for toluene and xylene were both below 38% and 26%, respectively. These values were significantly lower compared to those obtained from experiments using single solutions, indicating a potential competition between toluene and xylene molecules for the adsorption sites. Furthermore, the selectivity parameter calculated for this adsorption experiment was 0.67 for toluene, while for xylene, it remained at 0.31. This result suggests a preferential adsorption of toluene, which can be attributed to its specific properties.

Regarding the reuse cycle results, it is shown that the adsorption capacity of α-CD/CA/CDots in relation to toluene and xylene is minimally affected after ten adsorption/desorption cycles. For toluene (see Fig. S3(D)), the estimated values of q e varied by (10 ± 2)%, and for xylene (Fig. S3(E)), q e varied by (12.3 ± 0.2)%. These findings indicate that α-CD/CA/CDots is a stable and recyclable adsorbent for the removal of toluene and xylene from water. In operational aspects, this work provides a much more robust material, especially at real operating pH and equilibrium time of less than 30 min. Furthermore, it is a material that has an elevated level of sustainability. Bifunctional materials present circularity of use, reducing expenses to achieve a purpose.

Considering the characterization and adsorption data, we assert that the presence of CDots played a significant role in achieving these results. Moreover, the weak physical interaction forces established between the adsorbent's surface and aromatic molecules facilitated the desorption and regeneration process of post-used samples. A straightforward heating evaporation process proved sufficient for regenerating α-CD/CA/CDots, offering several advantages. Notably, its simplicity of use and recovery sets it apart from more sophisticated materials assessed for the same purpose.

Exploiting the high interaction capacity demonstrated by the α-CD/CA/CDots material with aromatic contaminants, in the following sections, we will apply it to the process of detecting these contaminants in the environment. The fluorescence properties attributed to the α-CD/CA matrix by the CDots will be utilized to observe the degree of quenching in the presence of each contaminant, while also assessing whether the contaminant concentration can influence these rates. Other fluorescence properties of the α-CD/CA/CDots material will be evaluated to employ it as a probe for such contaminants.

The optical properties of the α-CD/CA/CDots material were investigated using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. As shown in Fig. S4(A), the absorption spectrum of α-CD/CA/CDots exhibits a broad absorption band with a maximum at 214 nm and a prominent shoulder at approximately 267 nm, which, as reported elsewhere (Ahirwar et al., 2023; Silva et al., 2024) were assigned to the characteristic π–π* (core) and n–π* (shell) transitions, respectively, of the CDots dispersed in the α-CD/CA matrix.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the α-CD/CA/CDots collected at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S4(B). An excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior was observed for the α-CD/CA/CDots, which indicates a distribution of CDots sizes and/or the formation of different entrapments of the CDots with (α-CD/CA/CDots) (Gan et al., 2016). Moreover, the fluorescence spectrum exhibits asymmetric peaks, suggesting multifluorophores or luminescent species.

The fluorescence sensing stability of the α-CD/CA/CDots was evaluated under operable conditions of pH (4–8), temperature (10–50 °C), and material storage time (0–20 days). The pH-dependent studies showed that the emission intensity of α-CD/CA/CDots increases until pH 6, where it reaches its maximum, and from there decreases until pH 8, Fig. S4(A). As elsewhere described, this behavior is assigned to the lower degree of protonation of the O–H groups of the CDots as the pH increases (Taipale et al., 2022). Fluorescence temperature-dependent studies demonstrated that the emission intensity increases up to 30 °C and from then on, the fluorescence intensity stabilizes, Fig. S4(B). The stabilization of dynamic fluorescence is expected, due to the static collisional mechanism of the molecules that are aggregated to the matrix (Yu et al., 2012).

Furthermore, the α-CD/CA/CDots probes showed excellent photostability over 10 days, under 4 h of daily irradiation with UV light (Fig. S4(C)). The fact that their emission intensity underwent slight change after 10 uninterrupted days of experiment shows that the fluorescence sensing properties of this material has a long useful life and shows potential for reuse. It is worth mentioning that the stability observed for the α-CD/CA/CDots can be attributed to the anchoring of the CDots in the α-CD, which protects the surface of the CDots against damage.

To understand the sensor capacity of the α-CD/CA/CDots probe, its fluorescence response was measured in different concentrations of toluene and xylene (0–200 mg/L), maintaining the best experimental conditions (pH 6 and 25 °C). As shown in Fig. 6(A) and (B), increasing concentrations of aromatic contaminants produce a proportional quenching in the fluorescence of the α-CD/CA/CDots probe.

The Stern–Volmer plots for α-CD/CA/CDots in the absence and presence of the aromatic hydrocarbons shows a downward curvature, suggesting the presence of two fluorophore populations with one of them not being accessible to the quencher (Fig. 7 (A)) (Lakowicz, 2006; Mátyus et al., 2006). The accessible Stern–Volmer quenching constants for toluene (K
SVa = 15.4 M−1) and xylene (K
SVa = 10.3 M−1) were calculated from the Lehrer modified Stern–Volmer plot (see, Fig. 7(B)) (Sekowski et al., 2023). Moreover, the values lower than 1 for the fraction of initial fluorophores that are accessible to the quenchers, f
a = 0.83 and 0.71 (for toluene and xylene, respectively) gives further support for the existence of a population of fluorophores that are not accessible to the quenchers. These values are in close agreement with those reported by the kinetics analysis. The higher f
a value found for toluene when compared to xylene agrees with the higher removal efficiency observed for toluene in the adsorption studies. In addition, the higher binding constant value, log K
a , found from double-logarithmic plot (log(F
0
–F)/F vs. log [Q]) in Fig. 7(C) for toluene, further support the higher affinity of the α-CD/CA/CDots towards toluene (log K
a = 0.51 vs. 0.26 for xylene). Based on these data, the determined limits of detection (LOD) of α-CD/CA/CDots for toluene and xylene were 3.7 mg/L and 4.9 mg/L, respectively. Compared to the few examples reported in the literature, these LOD values are attractive and underscore the sensitivity of α-CD/CA/CDots regarding the tested VOCs. Zhu et al. (2024), employing Ag quantum dot-modified Co3O4, successfully detected toluene at 0.1 mg/L. In a separate study, Vassiltsova et al. (2007) modified CdSe quantum dots with pentafluorobenzoic acid, achieving LOD values for toluene and xylene of 50 mg/L and 15 mg/L, respectively.

Organic contaminants and biocontaminants are highly toxic and often show low solubility. Consequently, when they are soluble, they do not readily undergo degradation(Wang et al., 2014). This manuscript is centered on the development of a material intended not only for the efficient removal of such contaminants but also for their rapid detection in the medium. Prolonged contact and excessive absorption of these contaminants can result in bodily dysfunction, leading to neurological and respiratory diseases. The significance of detecting and removing these contaminants is thus justified, serving both the purpose of water quality control and the safeguarding of human beings, fauna, and flora.

In a more realistic context, contemporary systems for environmental remediation are becoming increasingly modern, portable, and require less trained personnel, aiming to reduce monitoring costs in remote locations. Consequently, the proposed material aligns with and is adaptable to this evolving reality. In a contact time of less than 30 min, a field analyst can not only generate a report on the presence of the contaminant but also remove approximately 70% of it from the sample using a minimal adsorbent dosage (1 mg/mL). In summary, the α-CD/CA/CDots composite, as presented in this study, proves effective in adsorbing both tested contaminants and shows discernible levels of fluorescent responses, enabling their detection in the aqueous medium.

This study presents the synthesis of a fluorescent composite of poly(α-CD) and CDots in a single experimental step, using CA as a crosslinker. The study aims to collaborate with studies focused on environmental remediation. In this way, the formulation of a material with adsorbent capabilities and which acts as a detector of volatile organic compounds, specifically xylene and toluene, was described. A material capable of removing and detecting organic compounds was efficiently presented, using cheaper techniques such as UV–vis spectroscopy. The CDots were efficiently entrapped in the α-CD/CA matrix and imparted distinctive physicochemical, morphological, and swelling properties to this composite compared to the pristine α-CD/CA sample. A series of experiments revealed that the α-CD/CA/CDots composite displayed enhanced adsorption capacity for two volatile organic compounds (VOCs), namely toluene and xylene. This novel adsorbent material showed notable decontamination capabilities, efficiently removing approximately 70% of these contaminants from water within just 24 min under mild experimental conditions. Due to its photoluminescent nature, α-CD/CA/CDots proved useful as a probe for detecting toluene and xylene in aqueous media with high sensitivity. In addition to proving the synthesis, characterization, and application of α-CD/CA/CDots as a valuable tool for water remediation and monitoring, this study opens new opportunities in the literature for potential advances related to CDots in areas of environmental remediation and composite research. Such advances are necessary, above all, in the application of detection in solutions of multiple contaminants and also in the process of adsorption and simultaneous detection in flow.
